items.title: Morphological classification of anemias
items.contentHtml:
Classification by MCV and MCHC

  • MCV (mean corpuscular volume): microcytic (<80 fL), normocytic (80-100 fL), macrocytic (>100 fL).
  • MCC (mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration): hypochromic (<32 g/dL), normochromic (32-36 g/dL).
  • Combinations: hypochromic microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of chronic disease in late stages); macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency, alcoholism, myelodysplasia, hypothyroidism).
  • The RDW (erythrocyte distribution width) helps: elevated in iron deficiency (anisocytosis), normal in thalassemia.

items.title: Iron deficiency anemia: diagnosis and treatment
items.contentHtml:
Iron deficiency anemia: low ferritin

  • Serum ferritin <15 ng/mL confirms iron depletion; in inflammation it can be falsely normal (use elevated soluble transferrin receptor).
  • Laboratory: hypochromic microcytic, low serum iron, high total iron binding capacity (TIBC), transferrin saturation <16%.
  • Causes: chronic loss (gastrointestinal, menstrual), poor absorption, diet.
  • Treatment: oral iron (ferrous sulfate 100-200 mg/day of elemental iron); reticulocyte response in 7-10 days, Hb normalizes in 6-8 weeks; continue 3-6 months after normalization to refill tanks.

items.title: Megaloblastic anemia: B12 and folate deficiency
items.contentHtml:
Megaloblastic anemia: B12/folate deficiency

  • Macrocytic (MCV >100 fL), with megaloblasts in bone marrow (nucleus-cytoplasm asynchrony) and neutrophil hypersegmentation (>5 lobes).
  • Vitamin B12 deficiency (cobalamin): causes: pernicious anemia, gastrectomy, ileitis, strict vegans; levels <200 pg/mL; It can cause neuropathy (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord).
  • Folate deficiency: alcoholism, pregnancy, drugs (methotrexate, trimethoprim); levels <4 ng/mL; without neuropathy.
  • Treatment: B12: cyancobalamin 1000 µg intramuscular/day for 7 days, then weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly for life; folate: folic acid 1-5 mg/day orally.

items.foco: Anemia of chronic disease: normal or high ferritin
items.title: Anemia of chronic disease (AEC)
items.contentHtml:
Anemia of chronic disease: normal or high ferritin

  • Normocytic normochromic (can be microcytic in late phases).
  • Pathophysiology: elevated hepcidin (due to IL-6) which sequesters iron in macrophages and reduces intestinal absorption.
  • Iron profile: normal or increased serum ferritin (>100 ng/mL), low serum iron, low or normal TIBC, low transferrin saturation (10-20%).
  • Differential diagnosis with iron deficiency: use soluble transferrin receptor (elevated in iron deficiency, normal in AEC) or soluble transferrin receptor/log index ferritin (>1.5 suggests added iron deficiency).
  • Treatment: treat underlying disease; does not respond to iron.

items.foco: Thalassemia: very low MCV with normal Hb
items.title: Thalassemia: thalassemic trait
items.contentHtml:
Thalassaemia: very low MCV with normal Hb

  • Heterozygotes (thalassemic trait): very low MCV (<75 fL) with normal or almost normal hemoglobin (11-14 g/dL) and normal RDW (contrary to iron deficiency).
  • Hemoglobin electrophoresis: in β-thalassemia minor: Elevated HbA2 (>3.5%); in α-thalassemia: normal (diagnosis by genetics).
  • Do not treat with iron; important to differentiate from iron deficiency to avoid unnecessary iron.
  • Mentzer formula: MCV/erythrocyte count (×10⁶/µL); <13 suggests thalassemia, >13 iron deficiency.

items.foco: Hemolytic anemia: high LDH and bilirubin
items.title: Hemolytic anemia: hemolysis data
items.contentHtml:
Hemolytic anemia: high LDH and bilirubin

  • Hemolysis: elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), high indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin (<25 mg/dL), reticulocytosis (>2% or >100,000/µL).
  • Smear: schistocytes (mechanical hemolysis), spherocytes (autoimmune), sickle cell (sickle cell), elliptocytes (hereditary).
  • Classification: intracorpuscular (membrane defect, enzymopathy, hemoglobinopathy) vs. extracorpuscular (autoimmune, mechanical, infectious, toxic).
  • Direct Coombs test positive in immune hemolysis.

items.foco: Direct Coombs test in autoimmune anemia
items.title: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA)
items.contentHtml:
Direct Coombs test in autoimmune anemia

  • Direct Coombs test (direct antiglobulin test) detects antibodies or complement bound to erythrocytes; positive in autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA).
  • Warm AHA (most common): IgG + C3d, response to corticosteroids (prednisone 1-2 mg/kg/day).
  • Cold AHA (cold agglutinin disease): IgM + C3d, avoid cold, rituximab or cyclophosphamide.
  • False negative: if the coating is low or IgA antibodies; use more sensitive techniques (gel, ELISA).

items.foco: Aplastic anemia: pancytopenia and hypocellular marrow
items.title: aplastic anemia
items.contentHtml:
Aplastic anemia: pancytopenia and hypocellular marrow

  • Pancytopenia (anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia) with hypocellular bone marrow (<25% normal cellularity) and absence of infiltration.
  • Causes: idiopathic (autoimmune), drugs (chloramphenicol, chemotherapy), viruses (hepatitis, parvovirus B19, HIV), radiation.
  • Diagnosis: bone marrow biopsy (hypocellularity, without blasts or fibrosis).
  • Treatment: allogeneic transplant of hematopoietic progenitors in young people with an HLA-identical donor; alternative: immunosuppression with antithymocyte globulin (ATG) + cyclosporine.

items.foco: Sickle cell anemia: sickle cell smear
items.title: Sickle cell anemia (sickling cell disease)
items.contentHtml:
Sickle cell anemia: sickle cell smear

  • Hemoglobinopathy due to mutation in the β-globin gene (Glu6Val): hemoglobin S (HbS).
  • Peripheral blood smear: sickling cells (sickle cells), dianocytes, Howell-Jolly bodies (due to asplenia functional).
  • Electrophoresis: HbS >90%, HbF (fetal) variable, HbA2 normal.
  • Crisis: painful (vaso-occlusive), aplastic (parvovirus B19), splenic sequestration, hemolytic.
  • Treatment: hydroxyurea (increases HbF, reduces crisis), folic acid, transfusions in severe crises; pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccines for asplenia.

items.title: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency
items.contentHtml:
G6PD deficiency anemia: oxidative hemolysis

  • X-linked enzyme deficiency; more common in men.
  • Triggers: oxidative stress (infections, drugs: sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, dapsone, primaquine, nalidixic acid, beans).
  • Acute hemolysis: anemia, jaundice, hemoglobinuria; smear: bite cells and Heinz bodies (denatured hemoglobin, visible with special stain).
  • Diagnosis: measurement of G6PD enzymatic activity (do not do during acute hemolysis because the reticulocytes have normal activity; repeat after 2-3 months).

items.title: pernicious anemia
items.contentHtml:
Pernicious anemia: anti-intrinsic factor antibodies

  • Atrophic autoimmune gastritis with deficiency of intrinsic factor (produced by gastric parietal cells).
  • Antibodies: anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (specific, 50-70% sensitivity) and anti-cell antibodies parietal (less specific, 90% sensitivity).
  • Laboratory: macrocytosis, vitamin B12 deficiency, elevated serum gastrin (due to achlorhydria).
  • Schilling test (rarely used): difference between intrinsic factor deficiency and poor intestinal absorption.
  • Treatment: cyancobalamin 1000 µg intramuscular for life (loading and maintenance dose).

items.title: Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS)
items.contentHtml:
Myelodysplasia: ring sideroblasts

  • Group of clonal disorders of the bone marrow with dysplasia in one or more lines (erythroid, myeloid, megakaryocytic) and peripheral cytopenias.
  • Ring sideroblasts (erythroblasts with >5 iron granules surrounding at least one third of the nucleus) define the subtype MDS with ring sideroblasts (MDS-SA).
  • WHO classification: according to dysplasia, blasts (<5% in marrow) and cytogenetics (5q deletion, monosomy 7).
  • Treatment: lenalidomide in 5q deletion; hypomethylating agents (azacytidine, decitabine) in high-risk MDS; curative allogeneic transplant.
